hero.title: Colonoscopy Companion
hero.subtitle: Your friendly guide to understanding and preparing for your colonoscopy
hero.description: Everything you need to know — from why it's recommended to what to expect on the day. Clear, trustworthy information to help you feel confident and prepared.
hero.reassurance: A colonoscopy is a safe, routine procedure — and you'll sleep right through it.

sections.title: What Is a Colonoscopy?
sections.subtitle: A simple look at the procedure
sections.summary: A colonoscopy is a safe, routine procedure that lets your doctor examine the inside of your colon. It takes about 30 minutes, and you sleep through it.

content.text: A colonoscopy is a procedure where a gastroenterologist (a doctor who specializes in the digestive system) uses a thin, flexible tube with a tiny camera to look at the lining of your large intestine (colon) and rectum.

points.heading: It's quick
points.text: The procedure itself usually takes 20 to 30 minutes. With preparation and recovery time, plan to be at the clinic for 2 to 3 hours total.

points.heading: You'll be comfortable
points.text: You'll receive sedation through an IV so you'll be relaxed or asleep during the procedure. Most people don't remember it at all.

points.heading: It's the gold standard
points.text: Colonoscopy is the most thorough way to examine the colon. It's the only screening test that can both find and remove polyps in the same session.

points.heading: It saves lives
points.text: Regular colonoscopy screening has been shown to reduce colorectal cancer deaths by detecting problems early, when they're most treatable.

content.heading: How it works
steps: You change into a gown and lie on your side
An IV is started and sedation medication is given
The colonoscope is gently inserted and advanced through the colon
The doctor examines the colon lining on a video screen
If polyps are found, they can be removed right away (painlessly)
The scope is withdrawn and you're taken to recovery

sections.title: Colon Cancer & Polyps
sections.subtitle: Why early detection matters
sections.summary: Colorectal cancer is the 3rd most common cancer in Canada, but it's one of the most preventable. Finding and removing polyps early stops cancer before it starts.

content.text: Colorectal cancer (CRC) develops in the colon or rectum. In most cases, it starts as a small growth called a polyp. Not all polyps become cancer, but removing them eliminates the risk.

content.heading: Colorectal cancer in Canada
items: It's the 3rd most commonly diagnosed cancer in Canada
About 1 in 14 Canadian men and 1 in 18 Canadian women will develop CRC in their lifetime
When found early (Stage I), the 5-year survival rate is over 90%
When found late (Stage IV), the 5-year survival rate drops to about 14%
Regular screening can prevent up to 60% of CRC deaths

content.heading: What are polyps?
content.text: Polyps are small growths on the inner lining of the colon. They're very common — about 25-40% of people over 50 have them.

items.name: Hyperplastic polyps
items.description: Usually small and found in the lower colon. These rarely become cancerous.

items.name: Adenomatous polyps (adenomas)
items.description: The most common type that can develop into cancer over time (usually 10-15 years). This is why they're removed during colonoscopy.

items.name: Sessile serrated polyps
items.description: Flat polyps that can be harder to spot. They have a higher potential to become cancerous and are an important reason for thorough colonoscopy.

content.heading: The polyp-to-cancer journey
content.text: Most colon cancers develop slowly from polyps over 10 to 15 years. This long timeline is exactly why screening works so well — there's a large window to find and remove polyps before they ever become dangerous. A colonoscopy interrupts this process by removing polyps during the exam.

sections.title: Screening Guidelines
sections.subtitle: When and how often to get screened
sections.summary: In British Columbia, colon cancer screening starts at age 50 for average-risk individuals. Your colonoscopy is covered by MSP at no cost when ordered by a physician.

content.text: Screening means checking for cancer or pre-cancerous polyps before you have any symptoms. Canada's guidelines recommend starting at age 50 for people at average risk.

points.heading: FIT every 2 years
points.text: The BC Cancer Colon Screening Program recommends a FIT (fecal immunochemical test) every 2 years starting at age 50.

points.heading: Positive FIT → colonoscopy
points.text: If your FIT test is positive, you'll be referred for a colonoscopy.

points.heading: Higher risk = earlier start
points.text: If you have higher risk factors, your doctor may recommend starting earlier or going directly to colonoscopy.

points.heading: Covered by MSP
points.text: Colonoscopy ordered by a physician is fully covered by MSP — there is no cost to you.

content.title: You may need earlier or more frequent screening if you have:
content.text: A parent, sibling, or child who had colorectal cancer or advanced polyps; a personal history of polyps or colorectal cancer; inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis); certain genetic conditions (Lynch syndrome, FAP); or symptoms such as blood in your stool, unexplained weight loss, or a change in bowel habits.

content.heading: How often do you need a colonoscopy?

items.interval: Starting at age 40 or 10 years before the youngest affected relative

sections.title: Prep Instructions
sections.subtitle: Your step-by-step preparation guide
sections.summary: Good preparation is the most important thing you can do for a successful colonoscopy. Follow these steps carefully for the best results.

content.text: The quality of your prep directly affects what your doctor can see. A clean colon means a thorough exam, fewer missed polyps, and less chance of needing to repeat the procedure.

content.heading: One week out checklist
items: Pick up your prep solution from the pharmacy if you haven't already
Stop taking iron supplements (they make the colon hard to see)
Arrange a ride home — you cannot drive after sedation
Stock up on clear liquids: broth, Jello, apple juice, sports drinks, popsicles

content.heading: Three days out checklist
items: Avoid high-fibre foods: raw vegetables, corn, nuts, seeds, whole grains, popcorn
Avoid red or purple liquids and Jello (they can look like blood during the exam)
Stick to low-residue foods: white bread, eggs, chicken, fish, cheese, yogurt

content.heading: The day before checklist
items: Switch to clear liquids only for the entire day
Begin drinking your prep solution as directed (usually in the evening)
Stay near a bathroom — the prep works quickly
Keep drinking clear fluids to stay hydrated
Your stool should become clear or light yellow by the end

content.heading: Procedure day checklist
items: Complete the second half of your split-prep (usually early morning)
Nothing to eat or drink after finishing your prep (or as directed)
Take essential medications with a small sip of water (ask your doctor which ones)
Wear comfortable, loose-fitting clothing
Bring your health card and a list of your medications
Your ride should be available to take you home after

content.heading: What counts as a clear liquid?
allowed: Black coffee or tea (no milk or cream)
avoid: Orange juice or any juice with pulp
Red or purple drinks of any kind

content.heading: Medications to discuss with your doctor
content.warning: Always confirm medication changes with your doctor. Never stop or change medications on your own.

items.note: Your doctor will tell you when to stop — usually 3-7 days before
Doses may need adjustment since you'll be fasting
Ask your doctor — low-dose ASA is usually continued

sections.title: What to Expect
sections.subtitle: Before, during, and after your procedure
sections.summary: Knowing what to expect helps reduce anxiety. Here's a step-by-step walkthrough of your colonoscopy day.

items: You'll check in at the endoscopy unit and change into a hospital gown
A nurse will review your medical history, medications, and allergies
An IV line will be placed in your arm for sedation and fluids
You'll meet your gastroenterologist and the care team
You'll sign a consent form — feel free to ask any questions
You'll lie on your left side on a stretcher
Sedation is given through your IV — you'll feel drowsy within moments
Most patients are asleep or very relaxed throughout the procedure
The procedure takes about 20-30 minutes
If polyps are found, they're removed painlessly during the exam
Tissue samples (biopsies) may be taken — also painless
You'll rest in the recovery area for 30-60 minutes as the sedation wears off
You may feel slightly bloated or gassy — this is normal and passes quickly
Your doctor will share the initial results before you leave
If biopsies were taken, results usually come back in 1-2 weeks
You can eat normally after the procedure (start light)
You must not drive, operate machinery, or make important decisions for 24 hours
Your ride home must be a responsible adult — taxis alone are not accepted at most centres

afterNote.heading: If polyps were removed
items: Avoid heavy lifting and strenuous exercise for 3-5 days
Do not take blood thinners or anti-inflammatory drugs until your doctor says it's safe
A small amount of blood in your first bowel movement is normal
Call your doctor if you have heavy bleeding, severe abdominal pain, or fever

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.subtitle: Answers to common concerns
sections.summary: Got questions? You're not alone. Here are answers to the most common things patients ask about colonoscopy.

items.question: At what age should most Canadians start colon cancer screening?
items.explanation: Current guidelines recommend starting colon cancer screening at age 45 for people at average risk. If you have a family history of colon cancer, screening may start earlier.

items.question: What is the most important part of colonoscopy preparation?
options: Avoiding solid food for a week
Drinking the bowel prep solution completely as directed
Taking extra laxatives the morning of
Eating a high-fiber diet the day before
items.explanation: Completing the full bowel prep as directed is the single most important thing you can do. A clean colon allows your doctor to see the entire lining clearly and find any polyps.

items.question: If a polyp is found during a colonoscopy, what does that mean?
options: It is usually removed during the procedure and most are not cancerous
items.explanation: Most polyps are benign (not cancerous) and are removed during the colonoscopy itself. Removing polyps is actually how colonoscopy prevents cancer — by catching and removing them before they can become a problem.

questions.a: No. You'll be sedated during the procedure, so you won't feel pain. Some people experience mild bloating or cramping afterward, which passes quickly. Most patients say the prep was the hardest part — not the procedure itself.

No. The sedation affects your judgment and reflexes for up to 24 hours, even if you feel fine. You must arrange for a responsible adult to take you home. Most endoscopy centres will cancel your procedure if you don't have a ride arranged.

Let your doctor know well in advance. You'll need to adjust your diabetes medications, especially insulin, since you'll be fasting. Monitor your blood sugar more frequently during the prep day. Clear liquids with some sugar (like apple juice or sports drinks) can help maintain your levels.

questions.q: What if my prep isn't working?
questions.a: Give it time — it can take 1-3 hours to start working. Walking around can help stimulate your bowels. Make sure you're drinking the prep as directed (not sipping too slowly). If you're vomiting or nothing is happening after several hours, call your doctor's office or the number on your prep instructions.

questions.q: How do I make the prep taste better?
questions.a: Chill the prep solution in the fridge. Drink it through a straw. Suck on a hard candy or lemon wedge between glasses. Some people find it easier to drink it quickly rather than sipping. Mixing prep with a clear flavoured drink is only okay if your specific prep instructions allow it.

questions.q: What medications should I stop before the procedure?
questions.a: This depends on your specific medications. Blood thinners usually need to be stopped 3-7 days before. Iron supplements should be stopped 7 days before. Always confirm with your doctor — never stop medications on your own.

If polyps are found, they're typically removed during the procedure — this is actually a good thing, as it prevents them from potentially becoming cancer. Your doctor will discuss the findings and any follow-up needed before you go home. Biopsy results, if taken, usually come back in 1-2 weeks.

questions.q: How often will I need another colonoscopy?
questions.a: It depends on what's found. If your colon is completely normal, you may not need another for 10 years. If polyps are found, your doctor will recommend a shorter interval (typically 3-5 years) based on the number, size, and type of polyps.

questions.q: Is a colonoscopy covered by MSP in BC?
questions.a: Yes. When a colonoscopy is ordered by a physician, it is fully covered by the BC Medical Services Plan (MSP). There is no out-of-pocket cost for the procedure itself. The prep solution is a prescription that you purchase at the pharmacy.

questions.q: Can I take the day off work?
questions.a: You should plan to take the full day off on your procedure day. Most people also prefer to be home the day before due to the prep. You can usually return to normal activities the day after your procedure.
